birthstone_status: October birthstone (as a variety of opal)
_all_text: Fire Opal fire-opal SiO2·nH2O Orange-red-yellow body color caused by iron oxide inclusions (hematite/goethite) and possibly organic compounds within the silica matrix. Some fire opals (precious fire opals) also show play-of-color in addition to the vivid body color. Fire opal's vivid orange-to-red body color makes it the only facetable opal variety (when transparent). The color evokes live flame, molten metal, and sunset. In Mexico it is called 'piedra de fuego' (stone of fire) and was sacred to the Aztecs as a symbol of Huitzilopochtli. Aztec civilization held fire opal sacred — the 'stone of the fire bird' associated with the hummingbird avatar of Huitzilopochtli. The Spanish conquest brought fire opal to Europe, where it was considered an exotic curiosity but never achieved the commercial success of the later Australian market. Mexico's fire opal tradition continues today — Querétaro produces the finest material and it is embedded in Mexican cultural identity. fire and passion solar energy creativity and desire the hummingbird spirit Mexican cultural identity Sacred stone of the Aztec civilization — 'quetzalitzlipyollitli' (stone of fire hummingbird) Used in Aztec ritual objects and jewelry Pre-Columbian Mesoamerican lapidary work Modern gem and jewelry use — faceted gems fire opal Mexican opal Querétaro opal piedra de fuego orange opal transparent opal faceted opal volcanic opal mineraloid variety opal silica amorphous volcanic orange red mexico aztec facetable october volcanic-fire aztec-hummingbird molten-glass-orange transparent-flame Querétaro-light orange red-orange yellow red
